coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots interfacial modified graphene/silicon Schottky barrier solar cell
coredata.dc:creator: Geng, Chao
Shang, Yudong
Qiu, JiaJia
Wang, Qidi
Chen, Xiuhua
Li, Shaoyuan
Ma, Wenhui
Fan, Hua-Jun
Omer, Altyeb-Ali-Abaker
Chen, Ran
coredata.dc:description:
The two-dimensional thin film, as an effective interface modification layer to improve the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of graphene/silicon (Gr/Si) heterojunction solar cells has attracted extensive attention recently. However, the complicated manufacturing process and specialized equipment requirements impede its broad application. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) fabricated under the mild reaction condition was first time used to modify Gr/Si devices interface structure. The effects of CQDs size distributions and coating thickness on J-V characteristics and the energy band structure of Gr/CQDs/Si solar cells were systematically analyzed. The results indicate that the PCE of resulting Gr/CQDs/Si solar cells could reach 9.97% when the thickness and sizes of CQDs interlayer are ∼26 nm and 4–7 nm, without any chemical doping, which is 6.8-times PCE than that of virgin Gr/Si solar cells. The CQDs interlayer serves as both an electron blocking layer and hole transport layer to reduce the carrier recombination, leading to a lower reverse saturation current as well as a larger V
OC then improved the device performance. The enhanced PCE shows that the CQDs species have the potential applications in a cost-effective photovoltaic device.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene/silicon solar cell
Carbon quantum dots
Interfacial modification
Energy band structure

Growth and transfer of graphene films
Graphene transfer using cyclododecane-based transfer method
Characterization of the carbon quantum dots(CQDs)
Performance analysis of the graphene/CQDs/n-Si type solar cells

J. Fan would acknowledge the partial financial support from the U.S. Department of Energy, National Nuclear Security Administration grant ( DE-NA 0001861 & DE-NA 0002630 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of graphene transferred by PMMA method (a) and cyclododecane method (b).
Transmission spectrum (a) and Raman spectrum (b) of graphene.
TEM characterization of CQDs fabricated at 200 °C, 225 °C, 250 °C, respectively.
TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images of representative CQDs fabricated at 200 °C.
(a) XPS full-scan survey spectra and (b) the high-resolution C1s spectra of CQDs.
PL spectra of CQDs fabricated at 200 °C, 225 °C, and 250 °C.

Schematic diagram (a) of the Gr/CQDs/Si solar cells. (b) Cross-sectional view, photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+) are driven by the built-in electric field into the n-Si and graphene electrode, respectively.
The dark lnJ-V curves (a) and Plots of dV/d(lnI) vs I (b) for Gr/CQDs/n-Si Schottky junction solar cell.

Light J-V curve (a) and Dark J-V curve (b) for Gr/CQDs/n-Si Schottky junction solar cells with 26 nm thickness; dark lnJ-V curves (c) and Plots of dV/d(lnI) vs I (d) for devices with three thickness interylaers.

UV–Vis spectrum (a), cyclic voltammetry curve (b) and the HOMO-LUMO energy level diagram of CQDs fabricated at 200 °C, 225 °C and 250 °C and schematic Energy band diagrams of the Gr/Si solar cells (a) without and (b) with CQDs electron blocking layer.
Flow chart of electron and hole migration in devices.
Light J-V curves (a) and variation of Jsc, FF (b) for Gr/CQDs/n-Si Schottky solar cell before and after ARC treatment. Distribution of Voc, FF and efficiency for several device samples (c).
Performance parameter of Gr/Si and Gr/CQDs/Si solar cells with different CQDs sizes.
Performance parameter of Gr/CQDs/Si solar cells with different CQDs thickness.
Performance parameters of Gr/CQDs/Si solar cells before and after ARC treatment.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots interfacial modified graphene/silicon Schottky barrier solar cell

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of New Energy/State Key Laboratory of Complex Nonferrous Metal Resources Clean Utilization
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The two-dimensional thin film, as an effective interface modification layer to improve the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of graphene/silicon (Gr/Si) heterojunction solar cells has attracted extensive attention recently. However, the complicated manufacturing process and specialized equipment requirements impede its broad application. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) fabricated under the mild reaction condition was first time used to modify Gr/Si devices interface structure. The effects of CQDs size distributions and coating thickness on J-V characteristics and the energy band structure of Gr/CQDs/Si solar cells were systematically analyzed. The results indicate that the PCE of resulting Gr/CQDs/Si solar cells could reach 9.97% when the thickness and sizes of CQDs interlayer are ∼26 nm and 4–7 nm, without any chemical doping, which is 6.8-times PCE than that of virgin Gr/Si solar cells. The CQDs interlayer serves as both an electron blocking layer and hole transport layer to reduce the carrier recombination, leading to a lower reverse saturation current as well as a larger V
OC then improved the device performance. The enhanced PCE shows that the CQDs species have the potential applications in a cost-effective photovoltaic device.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar cells, as one highly-efficient way utilizing the solar energy, have been studied since the early 1980s. The silicon material was the key player in solar cells because of its abundance, well-established processing techniques and the broad solar spectrum absorption range [1]. Although with the rapid prices decline in recent years, the high cost and complicated manufacturing processes are still hindering the large-scale applications of Si cell [2]. To solve these challenges, various new technologies have been developed with a more straightforward manufacturing route and lower cost [3,4]. Among them, graphene/silicon (Gr/Si) heterojunction devices have been considered as a potential alternative and been studied in recent years [5–7]. The first Gr/Si solar cell with a conversion efficiency of 1.5% was developed in 2010. Subsequently, various efforts were focused on improving the efficiency of Gr/Si solar cell [8–14]. The interface modification between Gr and Si is considered to be a highly effective way to improve the conversion efficiency. The introduction of the interlayer such as CH3NH3PbI3, h-BN, MoS2, HfO2, etc. is conducive to suppress the surface recombination process of carriers then enhance the performance of the Gr/Si-based solar cell [15–21].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) possess unique properties such as tunable band-gap, high conductivity and excitation wavelength-dependent emission, and long lifetimes of hot-carriers [22–24]. Furthermore, the CQDs are highly stable and nontoxic, especially the preparation of CQDs can be achieved through the solution synthesis process such as acidic exfoliation of pitch carbon fibers [25] or hydrothermal cutting of graphene sheets [26]. These relative mild reaction conditions ensure the cost-effectiveness and potential extensive applications of CQDs [27]. Recently, CQDs were applied in the fields of light-emitting diodes, novel solar cells, photodetectors and photocatalysis to improve the performance of photoelectric and energy devices [27–29]. The photoresponsivity of the graphene-silicon schottky junction photodetector has been enhanced by coupling the CQDs [30]. However, to the best of our knowledge, no study about CQDs modification of Gr/Si schottky solar cell has yet been reported.

In this study, an efficiency enhanced Gr/Si solar cell is prepared using CQDs as interfacial modification layer. Owing to the unique band structures and electrical transfer properties, CQDs interlayer can act as both electron blocking layer and hole transport layer in the Gr/CQDs/Si device. By fine-tuning the thickness and sizes of CQDs interlayer, an enhanced PCE of 9.97% was obtained for the proposed Gr/CQDs/Si cells under AM 1.5 G irradiation. The detailed energy band analysis was performed to explain the synergy mechanism of CQDs interlayer. The current work shows that the CQDs species have the potential applications in cost-effective photovoltaic devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs was prepared through direct pyrolyzing citric acid (C6H8O7) by carbonizing treatment [31]. Briefly, the process was the following: 2 g citric acid was placed into a hydrothermal synthesis reactor then the reactor was heated for 15 min in muffle stove (SX2-10-17) at 200, 225, and 250 °C, respectively. The resulting CQDs solution was dropped wisely added into 4 mg/mL NaOH solution under vigorously stirring. During this process, the color of mixture changed from colorless to pale yellow, and the final orange color. After adjusting the alkaline solution to neutral, a pure CQDs aqueous solution was obtained.

TiO2 nanoparticles was synthesized by the following process. Precursor reagent was prepared by mixing 0.5 mL of ethyl acetate, 23 mL of ethanol and 5 mL of Ti(OBu)4 in flask. The hydrolysate solution containing 12 mL ethanol, 1.5 mL HNO3(0.1 mol/L) and 1.5 mL de-ionized water was then infused by droplets into the precursor reagent at a rate of 0.5 mL/min. Quasi-TiO2 nanoparticles solution was stirred continuously for 12 h at 800 rpm under magnetic force, Finally, the TiO2 nanoparticles was obtained after 10 h aging processes at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth and transfer of graphene films
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene films were prepared on the copper substrate via the published plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition (PECVD) method [32,33]. The resulting graphene sheets were transferred onto the silicon wafer at room temperature. The mixed solution of cyclododecane and cyclohexane with mass ratio of 1:1 was spin-coated on the graphene films surface as supporting layer. The copper substrate was etched away by etchant of FeCl3:HCl:H2O = 10 g:10 mL: 90 mL. After etching process, the graphene/cyclododecane supporting layer was then thoroughly rinsed with DI water to remove the residues.

A n-type single Si<100> wafer with resistivity of 1–5 Ω cm−1 and 300 nm SiO2 oxidation layer, purchased from Hefei Crystal Material Technology Co. Ltd., was employed as the substrate for the graphene/CQDs/n-Si heterojunction solar cell. Surface contaminations on the Si wafer were removed via ultrasonic cleaning at room temperature in acetone, ethanol solutions and de-ionized water. A gold electrode was deposited onto the SiO2/Si substrate (window size of 3 × 3 mm2) by magnetron sputtering technique. The diluted HF solution (5%) removed the oxide layer to open the window, then was rinsed using de-ionized water.

Finally, the CQDs solution was spin-coated onto the opening window region, followed by annealing to remove the water and impurities, the construction of the Gr/CQDs/n-Si heterojunction was accomplished. The thickness of CQDs interlayer was controlled by spin-coating parameters, which spin-coating speed were 1000 rpm (for ∼40 nm), 3000 rpm (for ∼26 nm) and 4000 rpm (for ∼12 nm), respectively. The graphene was transferred to the top of the device by a new cyclododecane-based transfer method at room temperature [34]. In–Ga alloy was pasted on the rear side of the solar cell to form the back electrode. Devices were placed in ethanol vapor for 4 h to purge the surface. TiO2 nanoparticles were then spin-coated onto the graphene/CQDs/n-Si solar cells at 6000 rpm for 1 min to form a uniform anti-reflection layer with a thickness of 40–80 nm for light trapping [35].

Morphologies and structures of CQDs were characterized by TEM (JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan). Elemental composition and chemical bond type of CQDs films were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI). Photoluminescence (PL) and UV–vis spectra were measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (Ihr 320) and UV/vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550), respectively. Photovoltaic characteristics of the graphene/CQDs/n-Si solar cells were collected by Keithley 2612 in an ambient environment. Solar simulator equipped with a 300 W xenon lamp was used to produce AM 1.5G solar irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene transfer using cyclododecane-based transfer method
ce:section.ce:para: The transfer of plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposited graphene is a crucial process, which can affect the quality of the transferred sheets and compromise their application in devices. Generally, poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) was used as supporting layers during the copper etching. The heating treatment and organic solvents are required because PMMA is incompatible with many other polymeric substrates [36]. An improved graphene transfer method has been proposed by a previous report [37]. Fig. 1 shows SEM images of PMMA-transferred (a) and cyclododecane-transferred (b) graphene. The conventional PMMA transfer method has a high risk of contaminating the graphene surface, where the PMMA residue is observed, as shown in Fig. 1(a). This undesired contamination would increase the resistance and carriers recombination, which then reduces the short-circuit current [34,38]. The cyclododecane-transferred graphene has a much cleaner and smoother surface due to the strong sublimation ability of the protective cyclododecane supporting layer, which is volatile in ambient conditions.

Graphene was transferred to quartz glass to test the optical properties, and its transmittance property is shown in Fig. 2 (a). Transmittance spectrum of the graphene film is as high as 92% in visible light range, which fits well with the transmittance of three layers of graphene [39,40]. Raman spectrum of graphene has three determinate characteristic peaks of D peak (1350 cm−1), G peak (1590 cm−1) and 2D peak (2680 cm−1). The peak intensity ratio of 2D peak to G peak (I2D/1G) is 1.64 which further confirms the graphene film has about three layers structure, and the relative low D peak indicates the film has few defects [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the carbon quantum dots(CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Mild citric acid pyrolysis method is used to prepare the CQDs, in which different sizes can be obtained by adjusting reaction temperatures. TEM images of CQDs prepared at 200 °C, 225 °C and 250 °C were shown in Fig. 3 (a)-(c). The CQDs with sizes of 2–5 nm and 4–7 nm obtained at 200 °C and 225 °C exhibit good dispersion. The CQDs show an irregular shape and size of CQDs significantly increases to 7–12 nm when reaction temperature rises to 250 °C. Furthermore, the slight overlap and converging of CQDs is shown in Fig. 3(c), the poor-dispersed CQDs were likely caused by the excessive dehydration of citric acid and enhanced edge dangling bonds of the carbon atoms [42]. Fig. 4 is the TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) images of representative CQDs fabricated at 200 °C. The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm can be assigned as (1100) plane of graphite [43,44]. The corresponding selected lattice fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern is a regular hexagon and consistent with the (1100) plane of graphite, which further indicates that CQDs have high crystallinity [44].

Fig. 5 shows the XPS characterization on the CQD surface to confirm the functional groups. The full scan survey shows that CQDs mainly consists of carbon and oxygen elements and the binding energy of C1s and O1s are 284.8 eV and 531.2 eV, respectively. High-resolution spectra pattern of carbon elements reveals three different forms, which is assigned to sp2 (CC) with the binding energy of 284.7 eV, sp3 (C–C, and C–H) at 286.1 eV, and CO at 287.9 eV, as shown in Fig. 5(b). The existence of oxygen-containing functional groups suggests that the CQDs have excellent water solubility [42,45,46].

Fig. 6 shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CQDs prepared at 200 °C, 225 °C and 250 °C. PL spectra show that the fluorescence of CQDs strongly depends on the excitation wavelength, the intensity of the peaks is increased with the increasing excitation wavelength (Fig. 6(a), (b), (c)). The strongest peak appears at the 420 nm excitation-wavelength for the CQDs synthesized at 200 °C and 225 °C. For these two CQDs, the excitation wavelength range from 380 nm to 440 nm and 370 nm–440 nm, respectively. A red-shift occurred for the CQDs synthesized at 250 °C where the excitation wavelength ranges from 483 nm to 543 nm, and the strongest PL peak also appears at excitation wavelength of 410 nm (Fig. 6(c)), which is attributed to the photon re-absorption [47]. Fig. 6(d) plots the PL peak intensities of CDQs vs the excitation wavelength which demonstrate the wavelength-dependent excitation characteristics, and the most robust fluorescence is obtained when the CDQs is prepared at 225 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance analysis of the graphene/CQDs/n-Si type solar cells
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 7 displays the schematic structure of a Gr/CQDs/Si device. When light irradiates on the device surface, the valence electrons in silicon transfer to the conduction band by absorbing photon energy which create the photon-generated electrons and holes pair. Built-in electric field (V
bi ) between graphene and silicon interface separates the electron-hole pairs in the depletion region, graphene draws holes from the depletion region, while silicon draws electrons. Generally, graphene/silicon Schottky junction has a low energy barrier, which cannot effectively separate photon-generated carriers, resulting in a poor performance for a typical Gr/Si solar cell.

To investigate the effect of different CQDs size on Gr/CQDs/Si solar cells performance, the dark lnJ-V curves (a), plots of dV/d(lnI) versus I(b) for Gr/CQDs/Si Schottky junction solar cell fabricated based on series sizes have shown as Fig. 8 . At low forward voltages bias region (V < 0.13 V) in Fig. 8(a), a linear J-V relationship for three devices is obtained, which is consistent with the Ohm’s law [48]. At medium forward voltages bias region (V = 0.13–0.63 V), the dark J-V of devices is dominated by the thermionic emission model as expressed in equation (1) and (2)
(1)
J =
J 0
[ exp
(
q V
n k T
)
− 1 ]
(2)
J o =
A ∗
T 2
exp
(
− q
φ B
k T
)
Where k denotes Boltzmann constant; q is unit charge; T is absolute temperature; A∗ is effective Richardson constant (≈112 A cm−2 for n-Si); n is ideality factor; J
0 denotes saturation current density and φ B is the Schottky barrier. At higher forward voltage bias regions (V > 0.63 V), the slopes of dark ln(J)-V curves is reduced, and the curves deviate from the exponential relationship. The log-log plots of the J-V curves display power-law dependence in the range of ∼0.757 V, as shown in Fig. 8(a), which indicates that the current is confined by space-charge restricted current conduction of
J ∝
V M
[49,50], where M depends on the states density of CQDs interlayer and is estimated to be 1.9 ± 0.5.

When the thickness of the CQDs interlayer is 26 nm and with the size of 4–7 nm, the saturation current density J
0 of 8.05 × 10−9 A/cm2 can be obtained, which is about 15-times smaller than that of the virgin Gr/Si solar cell. According to the slope of dark ln(J)-V curves, the ideality factors were extracted to be 2.1 and 2.8 for solar cells without/with (4–7 nm) CQDs interlayer, respectively. The introduction of CQDs creates an enhancement in φ B and V
bi parameters.
(3)
V
O C
=
φ B
+
k T
q
ln
J
S C
A ∗
T 2

For Gr/CQDs/Si solar cell, V
OC can be expressed by Eq. (3), the value of V
OC is controlled both by the barrier height φ
B and J
SC . The φ B of Gr/Si and Gr/CQDs (4–7 nm)/Si samples were calculated to be 0.65 eV and 0.74 eV, respectively. All the devices express enhanced photovoltaic performance compared to the solar cells without CQDs interlayer, as shown in Table 1 . The V
OC is decreased with the increasing CQDs’ size, from 0.51V for 2–5 nm CQDs to 0.43V for 7–12 nm CQDs, which is attributed to φ B decrease from 0.74 eV to 0.71 eV. The CQDs with smaller size have a larger band gap, the V
OC is increased with the decreasing size. The hole transportation, however, is also increasing with the decreasing size, which is responsible for the decrease of J
SC (9.51 mA/cm2 for 2–5 nm, as shown in Table 1). Thus, an appropriate CQDs size of 4–7 nm was achieved to balance the J
SC and V
OC in current work.

Besides particle size, the effects of CQDs film thickness on the photovoltaic performance of Gr/CQDs/Si Schottky solar cells were also investigated in Fig. 9 . When the size parameter of CQDs interlayers is limited to 4–7 nm and the thickness is 12 nm, the V
OC calculated on the grounds of Eq. (3) shows an increment of 78 mV, which matches with the experimental increase (from 0.21V to 0.34V) of 130 meV. Voc, as well as FF, benefits from 26 nm ideal thickness, as shown in Table 2 . The non-uniform CQDs layer could be the main reason for relatively poor performance at thin thickness of 12 nm. The incomplete surface cover of CQDs interlayer leads to the low parallel resistance, causing a decrease in V
OC and FF, as shown in Table 2. However, the CQDs layer with an excessive thickness of 40 nm will absorb more light, therefore, aggravate the optical harvesting. Also, the carrier transport distance between graphene and silicon is increased once the CQDs layer is too thick, where the electron migration is hindered. The separation of electron-hole pairs at junction interface, therefore, will be deteriorated, leading the carrier recombination as well as the series resistance to be increased. Both of light-blocking effect and carrier recombination process is responsible for the performance degradation at large thickness of 40 nm, especially for the sharp decline of FF from 67.16% for 26 nm to 20.06%. The thin interlayer with a thickness of 12 nm has achieved large J
SC of 31.74 mA/cm2, while J
SC diminished since the thickness of CQDs was 26 nm, which attributes to the more substantial series resistance with the increasing thickness. When the interlayer thickness increases to 40 nm, the J
SC rebounded to 28.14 mA/cm2. The thick interface layers, especially for those exceeding 20 nm, they may absorb more light and produce some additional electron-hole pairs thus increases J
SC [30,31]. In summary, the optimum photovoltaic performance of solar cells is achieved at the CQDs interlayer thickness with 26 nm and size with 4–7 nm.

To investigate how CQDs interlayer affect the devices’ performance, band-gap energy of the prepared CQDs was estimated base on UV–Vis absorption spectrum (as shown in Fig. 10 (a)) and Tauc’s Law
(4)
B
A h v
+
E g
= h v
Where B denotes a constant; A denotes absorption coefficient; h is Planck’s constant; v is photon frequency and E
g is the band-gap energy. The optical band gaps of CQDs, therefore, can be extracted by plotting (
B
A h v
) vs. һυ then making a linear fitting, which are calculated as 3.36 eV, 3.01 eV, and 2.87 eV, respectively, which show size-dependent behavior.

The energy level structures of CQDs were investigated by a standard three-electrode system, consisting of a Ag/AgCl reference electrode, counter electrode and working electrode (CQDs covered platinum foil), and the supporting electrolyte is 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium hexafluorophosphate. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of CQDs were scanned from −1.0 V to +1.5 V. Fig. 10(b) shows the oxidation initiation potential (E
ox ) values of 0.95 eV, 0.88 eV, and 0.83 eV for CQDs fabricated at 200 °C, 225 °C, and 250 °C, respectively. The HOMO values can be respectively calculated as −5.35 eV, −5.28 eV, and −5.23 eV, based on the formula of E
HOMO = -e(E
ox + 4.4) [51], they are consistent with the previous research [52,53]. On the base of absorption curves, band-gap values of quantum dots are obtained as 3.36 eV, 3.01 eV, and 2.87 eV, respectively, and their corresponding LUMO values are respectively −1.99 eV, −2.27 eV, −2.36 eV, as shown in Fig. 10(c). The energy band diagrams of Gr/Si solar cells without (a) and with (b) CQDs interlayer are shown in Fig. 10(d) and (e).

In current Gr/CQDs/Si device, the photon-generated electrons are effectively prevented from entering graphene and Au electrode because of a large energy offset (ΔΦ = E C-LUMO, ca. 1.78eV for 4–7 nm CQDs), which benefit for restraining interface carrier recombination. The energy offset of E V-HOMO (ΔΦ, ca. 0.11eV for 4–7 nm CQDs) energy barrier can hinder the photon-generated hole transport from silicon to CQDs layer. Fortunately, the energy barrier of 0.11eV is low and much smaller than the E C-LUMO offset, and has no severe effect on the performance of the solar cell. There are two possible reasons for this experiment results. It is reported that the CQDs film has a high p-type conductivity in the air owing to the adsorption of moisture and oxygen molecules in surface [45,46]. Hence the photon-generated holes in silicon bulk can also transfer from the silicon to graphene through the CQD interlayer via the tunneling effect, rather than through the HOMO level [54], as shown in Fig. 10(e). Also, the intermediate energy levels, existing in CQDs interlayer band-gap likely produced by edge or surface defects, could allow hole transport from silicon to graphene. Therefore, the CQDs interlayer can work as both an electron blocking layer and hole transport layer then reduce the carrier recombination, leading to a lower reverse saturation current and larger V
OC therefore improved the performance of the device (see Fig. 11).

Moreover, when the CQDs layer is introduced to Gr/Si solar cells, the ideality factor is not only affected by interfacial recombination but also controlled by interlayer thickness [55]. The ideality factor of Gr/CQDs/Si solar cells is raised from 2.70 to 3.13 and 4.17 with the reduction of CQDs size from 7-12 nm to 4–7 nm and to 2–5 nm due to the decrease of HOMO level. The increase of n factor also could produce an increase in V
OC (as shown in Table 1), which is similar to the previous report on Au/SiO2/n-Si device [56]. Meanwhile, the values of n and V
OC increase with an increasing interlayer thickness (δ) from 12 nm to 26 nm, but the minority carrier tunneling current will be obstructed once δ continually increases to 40 nm. Therefore, the optimal value of n factor is determined as 3.13 for current Gr/CQDs/Si solar cells. On the other hand, the planar silicon substrate has a high reflectivity of about 30–40% in the visible range. The application of anti-reflection coating (ARC) on Gr/Si-based solar cells using TiO2 nanoparticles to reduce the reflectance has discussed by Shi et al. [35]. For Gr/CQDs/Si solar cells, with optimal thickness of ∼26 nm and particle size of 4–7 nm, V
OC has a slight increase of 30 mV, J
SC increases from 19.04 mA/cm2 to 29.33 mA/cm2, whereas FF decreases from 67.16% to 66.59% (as shown in Table 3 and Fig. 12 ). The conversion efficiency of nano-TiO2 coated Gr/CQDs/Si solar cells increases from 6.52% to 9.97%, which is 6.8-times more than that of virgin Gr/Si solar cells, as shown in Fig. 12 (c). The devices with an ARC and 40 nm-thick CQDs interlayer have a significant increase to 3.42% by spin-coating nano-TiO2. Meanwhile, the Voc increases from 0.34 V to 0.41 V, J
SC increases slightly from 28.14 mA/cm2 to 29.31 mA/cm2, fill factor increases from 20.06% to 27.93%, as shown in Fig. 12(c). By comparing the work reported by Kim et al. [9], the PCE of current devices could be further improved by and using chemical doping such as nitric acid. The current results confirmed that the further PCE improvement of novel Gr/Si Schottky devices is desirable through spin-coating anti-reflection layer and interface energy band control.

In summary, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were fabricated via the mild reaction condition and were the first time introduced to modify the interface of Gr/Si solar cells. Due to its unique band structure, CQDs interlayer serves as both an electron blocking and hole transport layer, leading to a lower reverse saturation current and larger V
OC which improve the photovoltaic performance of the device. Compared with devices without CQD interlayer, the PCE of Gr/CQDs/Si solar cells was significantly enhanced at 9.97% with CQDs interlayer thickness of 26 nm and size of 4–7 nm. This PCE with modification is 6.8-times better than that of virgin Gr/Si solar cells. Given the cost-effective device manufacturing process, the CQDs interlayer will offer a promising prospect in interfacial modification of low-cost photovoltaic devices.

Chao Geng: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Yudong Shang: Conceptualization. JiaJia Qiu: Software. Qidi Wang: Software. Xiuhua Chen: Funding acquisition, Visualization, Investigation. Shaoyuan Li: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Wenhui Ma: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Hua-Jun Fan: Funding acquisition. Altyeb-Ali-Abaker Omer: Methodology. Ran Chen: Writing - review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support of this work from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 61764009, 51762043, 51974143), Key Project of Yunnan Province Natural Science Fund (2018FA027), Major Science and Technology Projects in Yunnan Province (No. 2019ZE007), Yunan Ten Thousand Talents Plan Young & Elite Talents Project and the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Ministry of Education of China (No. IRT_17R48), H. J. Fan would acknowledge the partial financial support from the U.S. Department of Energy, National Nuclear Security Administration grant (DE-NA 0001861 & DE-NA 0002630).
